root_cause.text: The `DefaultHttpFirewall` did not restrict URL-encoded slash characters (`%2f` or `%2F`), allowing attackers to bypass security constraints due to discrepancies in how different servlet containers and Spring MVC decode and process path parameters.
root_cause.evidence: The patch adds `containsInvalidUrlEncodedSlash(requestURI)` to reject requests containing `%2f` or `%2F`. The test comments explicitly note: "On WebSphere 8.5 a URL like /context-root/a/b;%2f1/c can bypass a rule on /a/b/c because the pathInfo is /a/b;/1/c which ends up being /a/b/1/c while Spring MVC will strip the ; content from requestURI before the path is URL decoded."

agent_logs.root_cause_candidate: The `DefaultHttpFirewall` did not restrict URL-encoded slash characters (`%2f` or `%2F`), allowing attackers to bypass security constraints due to discrepancies in how different servlet containers and Spring MVC decode and process path parameters.
agent_logs.evidence_points: The patch adds `containsInvalidUrlEncodedSlash(requestURI)` to reject requests containing `%2f` or `%2F`. The test comments explicitly note: "On WebSphere 8.5 a URL like /context-root/a/b;%2f1/c can bypass a rule on /a/b/c because the pathInfo is /a/b;/1/c which ends up being /a/b/1/c while Spring MVC will strip the ; content from requestURI before the path is URL decoded."

intent_record.intent_summary: Reject requests containing URL-encoded slashes by default to prevent security vulnerabilities.
intent_record.what: Added a new field `allowUrlEncodedSlash` with a setter, and updated `getFirewalledRequest` to check the request URI for URL-encoded slashes (`%2f` or `%2F`) using a new helper method, rejecting the request if found.
intent_record.how: By default, the firewall will now reject incoming HTTP requests that contain URL-encoded slashes in their URI. This behavior can be overridden by calling `setAllowUrlEncodedSlash(true)`.

intent_record.intent_summary: Format the 'else if' statement to conform to standard Java styling guidelines.
intent_record.what: The 'else if' statement was moved to the same line as the preceding closing brace.
intent_record.how: This change has no effect on the runtime behavior of the program, as it only alters the whitespace and line breaks.

intent_record.intent_summary: Add tests to verify the handling and rejection of URL-encoded slashes in DefaultHttpFirewall.
intent_record.what: Added new unit tests to DefaultHttpFirewallTests to check handling of URL-encoded slashes, and applied minor code formatting.
intent_record.how: Verifies that DefaultHttpFirewall rejects requests with lowercase and uppercase URL-encoded slashes by default, and allows them when the allowUrlEncodedSlash property is set to true.

grouping_result.intent: Reject requests containing URL-encoded slashes by default to prevent security vulnerabilities.
Format the 'else if' statement to conform to standard Java styling guidelines.
Add tests to verify the handling and rejection of URL-encoded slashes in DefaultHttpFirewall.

relevance_review.reason: The group directly addresses the vulnerability by rejecting requests containing URL-encoded slashes, which was the exact root cause of the security bypass.
The group only contains formatting and style changes, which do not address the vulnerability regarding URL-encoded slash characters.
The group adds tests to verify the rejection of URL-encoded slashes in DefaultHttpFirewall, directly addressing the vulnerability root cause.

candidates.rationale: This line checks if the path is normalized but fails to check for URL-encoded slashes (%2f or %2F), allowing requests with these characters to bypass the firewall.
The method processes the incoming request and is responsible for rejecting malicious paths, but it lacked the specific logic to reject URL-encoded slashes.

vuln_statements.explanation: This statement represents the existing security checks on the path, which failed to include a check for URL-encoded slashes (%2f or %2F), allowing the bypass.

bic_trace_step.reason: The DefaultHttpFirewall implementation shown in the diff checks if paths are normalized (e.g., rejecting '.' and '..') but does not contain any logic to restrict URL-encoded slash characters ('%2f' or '%2F'), which is the root cause of the vulnerability.

    SEC-1584: Addition of HttpFirewall strategy to FilterChainProxy to reject un-normalized requests and wrap the incoming request object before processing by the security filter chain to provide a more consistent representation of paths than is guaranteed by the servlet spec. The wrapper strips path parameters from pathInfo and servletPath to provide consiste
bic_trace_step.reason: The newly introduced DefaultHttpFirewall checks for directory traversal sequences ('.' and '..') in the isNormalized method, but it does not check for or restrict URL-encoded slash characters ('%2f' or '%2F'), leaving it vulnerable to security bypasses.
